skill.name: meituan-printer
skill.description: Manage and print formatted task slips to Meituan thermal printers over a local network. It supports printer aliases, a local web admin UI, print templates, and scheduled printing.
skill.summary: Print and schedule Meituan thermal printer task slips from Claude, Codex, or Claude Code.
skill.author: chenyangshu

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, script, and persistence indicators. Review confirmed that many documentation and byte-literal detections are false positives, but the skill includes a Flask admin server bound to all interfaces with no authentication and endpoints that can run print jobs, write local configuration, and export OS scheduled tasks. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found, but publication should be blocked until authentication, binding, and scheduler controls are hardened.

high_findings.title: Unauthenticated Network-Exposed Admin Interface
high_findings.description: The Flask admin app binds to 0.0.0.0 and exposes APIs that manage printers, tasks, templates, and immediate print execution without authentication. Any host that can reach the service may modify local printer configuration or trigger printer actions.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The server explicitly listens on all interfaces and the reviewed routes do not check authentication. The affected routes call subprocess-based print execution and system task export.

high_findings.title: Operating System Scheduled Task Persistence
high_findings.description: The skill can write macOS LaunchAgents and load them with launchctl, and can create Windows scheduled tasks with schtasks. This is documented as a feature, but it creates persistent execution outside the AI session and needs explicit user consent and hardening.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly writes LaunchAgent files and invokes launchctl and schtasks. The behavior is intentional, but it is a confirmed persistence capability.

high_findings.title: Windows Scheduled Command Injection Risk
high_findings.description: The Windows scheduler command is built as one quoted command string using task title and content. Quote characters or shell metacharacters in scheduled task content may alter the command that Task Scheduler later executes.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The task payload is user supplied through the web API and interpolated into a Windows command string. The exact exploitability depends on Windows command-line parsing, so confidence is high but not absolute.

medium_findings.title: DOM XSS Risk in Local Admin UI
medium_findings.description: The frontend writes toast messages and rendered tables through innerHTML. Some messages include user-controlled aliases or server response text, which may allow script injection in the browser admin UI.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code has an escaping helper and uses it in several table cells, but showToast inserts raw message HTML. User-controlled aliases are included in displayed messages.

medium_findings.title: Arbitrary Local Network Printer Connections
medium_findings.description: The print script accepts a direct IP and port and opens a TCP socket to send ESC/POS commands. This is core functionality, but it can be abused for unwanted network traffic or unauthorized printing if exposed through the admin UI.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The socket behavior is direct and intentional. It is legitimate for a printer skill, but it becomes risky when combined with unauthenticated web access.

medium_findings.title: Unauthenticated Local File Writes
medium_findings.description: The web backend writes printer configuration, task data, printer types, and templates based on API input. File locations are fixed, but the lack of authentication allows reachable clients to change operational data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The writes are constrained to known JSON files and use structured JSON serialization. The risk comes from unauthenticated access to the write APIs, not arbitrary path traversal.

medium_findings.title: Automatic Dependency Installation at Startup
medium_findings.description: The startup scripts install Flask and APScheduler with pip when imports fail. This is convenient, but it performs network package installation without pinning versions or verifying package integrity.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts clearly invoke pip install for two dependencies. This is a common setup pattern, but unpinned installation increases supply-chain risk.

low_findings.title: Documentation Command Examples Flagged as Execution
low_findings.description: Many static external-command findings are Markdown command examples or inline code in README and SKILL documentation. They are not executed by the files themselves, so these specific documentation detections are false positives.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are fenced Markdown examples, not Ruby backticks or runtime command execution. Separate script files contain the real execution risks.

low_findings.title: ESC/POS Byte Literals Flagged as Obfuscation
low_findings.description: Hex byte literals in the print script are ESC/POS printer control commands for alignment, font size, line feeds, and paper cutting. They do not appear to hide executable payloads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The byte literals are adjacent to comments and printer text encoding logic. Their semantic use matches thermal printer control sequences.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Private Printer Addresses
low_findings.description: Example and reference files include private RFC1918 printer IP addresses. These are not public endpoints, but they may expose real store network details and should be replaced with placeholders before publication.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The addresses are private local-network addresses used as examples or store references. They are not exfiltration endpoints, but they can disclose environment details.

dangerous_patterns.title: Flask Server Bound to All Interfaces Without Authentication
dangerous_patterns.description: The backend starts Flask on 0.0.0.0 and the reviewed API routes do not enforce authentication or CSRF protection.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The bind address and route implementations are explicit. No authentication middleware or per-route checks were found in the reviewed backend.

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess Execution from Web-Controlled Actions
dangerous_patterns.description: The backend uses subprocess.run for print execution and system task management. Arguments are mostly list-based, but they are reachable through web-controlled task operations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are direct and confirmed. Use of argv lists reduces shell injection risk, but unauthenticated web reachability keeps the pattern dangerous.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent Scheduler Export
dangerous_patterns.description: The code can install operating system scheduled tasks that continue running after the web service stops.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Both the implementation and documentation describe persistent system-level scheduling. This is not hidden, but it is a high-impact capability.

dangerous_patterns.title: Raw HTML Rendering of Dynamic Messages
dangerous_patterns.description: The frontend renders dynamic messages with innerHTML, including values that may include user-supplied aliases or backend error text.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The helper uses innerHTML for toast rendering and callers pass dynamic strings. Other render paths escape values, so the risk is concentrated in message rendering.

content.value_statement: Restaurant teams need reliable task slips without editing printer commands by hand. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code configure local printers, send formatted slips, and schedule recurring print jobs.
actual_capabilities: Sends formatted task slips to Meituan thermal printers over TCP using ESC/POS commands.
Selects printers by configured Chinese aliases or direct IP and port values.
Provides a local Flask web interface for printer, task, and template management.
Tests printer connectivity before adding or using a configured printer.
Creates daily, weekly, and monthly scheduled print tasks with APScheduler.
Exports scheduled tasks to macOS LaunchAgents or Windows Task Scheduler.
limitations: Requires Python and local network access to compatible Meituan thermal printers.
The web admin service has no built-in authentication in the reviewed version.
Printer output depends on GB18030 and ESC/POS support in the target device.
System task export changes operating system scheduler state and may need user approval.

use_cases.title: Print Shift Checklists
use_cases.description: Create and print morning, evening, or station checklists for restaurant staff.

use_cases.title: Manage Printer Aliases
use_cases.description: Map kitchen, vegetable, and front desk printers to clear local aliases.

use_cases.title: Schedule Recurring Task Slips
use_cases.description: Set recurring print jobs for daily preparation, cleaning, or inventory routines.

prompt_templates.title: Print a Simple Task Slip
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for one immediate print job.
prompt_templates.prompt: Print a task slip to the kitchen printer. Title it Morning Prep and include these lines: check freezer temperature, prepare vegetables, confirm booking count.

prompt_templates.title: Add a Printer Alias
prompt_templates.scenario: Configure a new local thermal printer.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me add a new Meituan printer alias named Front Desk. The IP is 192.168.1.50 and the port is 9100. Test the connection first.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Scheduled Print
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a daily 09:00 print task for the kitchen printer. The title is Opening Checklist and the content should cover stock, sanitation, and equipment checks.

prompt_templates.title: Review Printer Setup and Risks
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my configured printers and scheduled tasks. Identify stale aliases, duplicated schedules, and any system-level scheduled tasks that need confirmation.

output_examples.input: Print a test slip to the back kitchen printer.
output_examples.output: The assistant confirms the target printer alias, sends the formatted test slip, and reports whether the printer accepted the connection.

output_examples.input: Schedule the closing checklist every night at 22:00.
output_examples.output: The assistant creates a recurring task with the selected printer, title, checklist text, and the next planned run time.

output: A table of printer aliases, IP addresses, ports, printer types, and remarks.
A warning if no printer configuration exists yet.

best_practices: Bind the web admin interface to localhost unless remote access is explicitly required.
Confirm the target printer and content before sending or scheduling any print job.
Use placeholder IP addresses in shared examples and keep real store network details private.
anti_patterns: Do not expose the web admin service to a shared network without authentication.
Do not export system scheduled tasks without clear user approval.
Do not place secrets, customer data, or sensitive business records in printable templates.

faq.question: What printers does this skill target?
faq.answer: It targets Meituan thermal printers that accept ESC/POS commands over a local TCP connection.

faq.question: Can it print without the web interface?
faq.answer: Yes. The command-line script can print by printer alias or direct IP and port.

faq.question: Does it support scheduled printing?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports daily, weekly, and monthly schedules through the local web service.

faq.question: Can scheduled tasks run after the web service stops?
faq.answer: Yes, if the user exports them to macOS LaunchAgents or Windows Task Scheduler.

faq.question: Is the reviewed version safe to expose on a network?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed web admin service has no authentication and should not be exposed beyond localhost.

faq.question: Does the skill need internet access?
faq.answer: Printing uses the local network. Startup scripts may use internet access to install missing Python packages.
